text: Leakage in reservoirs located in karst regions is one of the main engineering geological issues in the construction of hydraulic hubs in soluble rock areas. This is particularly significant in regions where soluble rocks are widespread, karst development is intense, and the terrain is complex; in such areas, karst leakage has become a common problem for hydraulic and hydropower projects. The karst leakage in reservoir areas not only affects the normal water storage after dam construction but also jeopardizes the safety and stability of the dam itself. Therefore, it is necessary to conduct a detailed analysis of the leakage mechanisms, characteristics, and conditions specifically pertaining to leakage in reservoir areas, particularly in karst regions, and to propose suitable treatment measures for this leakage in order to ensure the optimal benefits of the reservoir are realized.

It is well known that after a dam is filled, the hydraulic pressure of the reservoir can lead to leakage phenomena in the dam body, foundation, shoulders, and neighboring valleys, which is detrimental to both dam safety and reservoir efficiency. According to statistics from abroad, dams that have experienced failure due to leakage problems account for 40.5% of the total failure incidents. The Ministry of Water Resources of China has categorized and organized 1,000 engineering accidents that occurred in 241 large reservoirs over more than 20 years since the founding of the country. Among these, leakage incidents (including piping) numbered 317, accounting for 31.7% of the total accidents. Therefore, assessing whether leakage is normal is an important indicator for evaluating the safe operation of a reservoir. The occurrence of reservoir leakage must be predicated on the corresponding geological and hydrogeological conditions, specifically the hydraulic conditions and pathways. From the perspective of the conditions for reservoir leakage and based on leakage pathway analysis, the most complex and prevalent leakage issue is karst leakage through karst conduits, whereas leakages through other pathways are generally simpler or have limited scales, and in some cases, the leakage volume can be negligible. Karst leakage is currently the predominant type of reservoir leakage. This is primarily determined through a comprehensive analysis of the geological characteristics of the area, including the lithology, distribution of geological structures, degree of karst development in soluble rock exposures, and hydrogeological characteristics. This analysis aims to ascertain whether significant changes will occur in the groundwater recharge and discharge relationships in the watershed area after reservoir filling, leading to permanent karst leakage. Extensive studies and analyses of karst leakage issues in reservoir areas have been conducted both domestically and internationally for a long time, and reservoirs have been successfully constructed in karst regions. However, there are cases where severe leakage has occurred due to inadequate geological investigations or incomplete anti-seepage measures in certain areas. Leakage is typically classified into two categories based on the pathway: leakage through dam segments in the reservoir area and reservoir leakage itself. The former includes foundation leakage and dam bypass leakage, while reservoir leakage encompasses leakage towards neighboring valleys or downstream discharge areas, or through river bends or tributary valleys.

The leakage from the dam foundation in the reservoir area. The karst leakage at the foundation not only causes water loss from the reservoir, reducing its efficiency, but also increases uplift pressure on the dam base, erodes fine particles in the soil and rock, or chemically dissolves soluble components within the soil and rock, leading to potential internal erosion of the dam foundation and instability of the foundation soil. Reservoirs such as the Cuijia Gou Reservoir in Bijie City, the Dahua Water Reservoir, and the Gaoyan Reservoir all experience dam foundation leakage. This leakage issue has plagued these reservoirs for many years; despite significant reductions in leakage quantity due to remedial measures, a small amount of reservoir water continues to flow out through the karst aquifer under the dam foundation, with no complete solution achieved.

Leakage around the dam section of the reservoir. Leakage around the dam is one of the primary geological issues in reservoir engineering, as it can lead to instability in the rock mass of the dam shoulder, jeopardizing dam safety, and significant leakage can diminish the reservoir's effectiveness. Therefore, analyzing leakage around the dam and calculating the leakage volume is particularly important. For nearly 70 years of operation, the Dahe Reservoir in Liaoning Province has been troubled by leakage issues around the dam. In 1994, grouting treatments were performed, which had certain benefits; however, the leakage phenomena on both banks remain severe, with many points of seepage exposed on both sides and considerable water loss from the reservoir. Additionally, the Hetian Reservoir and the Gypsum Mountain Reservoir in Lüliang City also experience leakage around the dam. Following treatment, the leakage volumes have decreased substantially, and research has indicated that a single waterproof curtain may not fully resolve leakage issues. It is advisable to adopt a combination of various impermeability measures based on specific engineering and hydrogeological conditions.

Leakage from the reservoir to the adjacent valley has been a significant issue troubling karst reservoirs. Due to the thin ridge watershed and the hydraulic gradient created by the reservoir's water level, the reservoir water is prone to leak along permeable rock layers and their leakage pathways into the adjacent valley. This type of leakage can only be avoided when the rock layers are steeply inclined, and the permeable layers below the reservoir water level extend below the valley floor of the adjacent valley. When the adjacent valley is deeply incised and the water level is lower than the reservoir water level, the thin ridges, passes, and watershed conditions around the reservoir shorten the leakage pathways, potentially resulting in a significant leakage volume. Leakage from the reservoir may trigger new landslides in the adjacent valley or reactivate ancient landslides, leading to flooding, salinization, and marsh formation, endangering agricultural production and the safety of homesteads. For instance, the Maojiadian Reservoir, Zipingpu Reservoir, and the Guankou Reservoir in Chengdu have all experienced leakage into adjacent valleys, causing certain harms to those areas. Other forms of reservoir water leakage also rely on the aforementioned necessary leakage conditions; as long as there is a hydraulic gradient, basic hydrodynamic conditions exist, along with permeable rock-soil bodies and their pathways providing runoff routes, reservoir leakage will occur. For example, the Musuo Reservoir in Jinsha County, Guizhou Province, leaks to distant discharge zones, while the Hongyang Reservoir in Zhi Jin County leaks downstream through river bends or tributary valleys, and these various leakage methods all impact the water retention capacity of the reservoirs.

With the development of water conservancy and hydropower projects, the number of dams constructed domestically is increasing; however, sites with favorable geological conditions are becoming scarcer. Currently, many dams are being built in regions with karst developments, necessitating careful foundation treatment and seepage control in the reservoir periphery and seepage pathways, with construction methods being relatively complex. This paper takes the Aikou Reservoir project as a research background and presents a comprehensive analysis of seepage issues in reservoir areas within karst regions, yielding the following key findings: 

1. There are generally two pathways for external seepage from the reservoir area: seepage from the reservoir itself and seepage from the dam area. Regardless of the form of seepage, there are specific geological conditions present; in karst-prone areas, the seepage volume from the reservoir mainly relates to the characteristics of karst development. 

2. Regarding the measures for controlling seepage in reservoir areas, the methods include: (1) geotextile blanket for seepage control; (2) concrete cover for seepage control; (3) cement mortar grouting in masonry joints for seepage control; (4) cement grouting and cement flushing of voids; (5) concrete cutoff walls; (6) seepage control curtain grouting, among which the seepage control curtain grouting method is particularly suitable when considering the conditions of the Aikou Reservoir project.

3. Field tests of grouting in the Aikou Reservoir project demonstrated that the implementation of consolidation and curtain grouting operations effectively improved the seepage resistance of the dam foundation. Additionally, these findings provide important parameters for the implementation of seepage control measures at the Aikou Reservoir, including unit cement consumption and curtain thickness and grouting hole numbers; layouts and construction procedures for grouting holes; hole spacing, row spacing, orientation, and depth; types of grouting materials and slurry concentration; and grouting pressure.

The issue of seepage in reservoir areas within karst regions has long been a focal point and challenge in hydraulic engineering, possessing significant practical significance and theoretical value, with broad application prospects. The research outcomes of this paper have been directly applied in the Chongqing Aikou Reservoir project, providing direct theoretical references and guidance for the design, construction, and management of practical and similar engineering projects.

After the reservoir is completed and filled, a water head difference occurs when the upstream water level is higher than the downstream water level, leading to seepage in the dam (dam body and foundation) under the influence of the head difference. To ensure the safety of the dam and optimize water storage efficiency, one of the critical design aspects is to address potential seepage issues in the reservoir area. Seepage of water from the reservoir generally occurs through two pathways: reservoir seepage and dam seepage. Reservoir seepage occurs when the area has suitable topography, structure, lithology, and hydrogeological conditions. Assessing whether seepage will occur in the reservoir area involves a comprehensive analysis of three main aspects: geomorphological conditions, lithology and geological structural conditions, and hydrogeological conditions. 

If the surrounding mountains of the reservoir are thin, with adjacent areas being cleared land or depressions, and the reservoir height and elevation are below the normal water level, then when a seepage pathway exists between the reservoir and neighboring areas, water from the reservoir may leak toward the vicinity. Similarly, in bedrock areas, seepage can occur if there are karst pathways in the watershed or bay zones, significant fault zones, fold and hinge areas, and well-developed joints in permeable rock layers. The aforementioned conditions are necessary for reservoir water to seep into neighboring or adjacent low-lying areas.

Thus, in determining whether seepage occurs in the reservoir area, one must first observe the topographical features on both banks of the river valley, such as thin watersheds, passes, river bays, and adjacent depressions. Next, the presence of seepage pathways (e.g., permeable rock layers, fault zones, karst pathways, etc.) in these regions should be identified, followed by an assessment of their connectivity based on geological structures. Finally, a comprehensive analysis of the hydrogeological conditions (such as the burial and movement characteristics of groundwater) will provide an evaluation of the seepage situation in the reservoir area.

Reservoir subsidence can induce leakage, primarily involving three aspects: 1. The vacuum negative pressure caused by the drawdown of reservoir water can lead to karst collapse and water leakage within the reservoir. For example, at the Shuishipo Reservoir in Puding County, Guizhou, when the reservoir water level decreases, underground karst pathways drain rapidly, resulting in scouring and vacuum suction that reduce water and air pressure, causing the formation of a collapse funnel within the reservoir that leads to external leakage. 2. When the reservoir water level rises, the lag in the increase of the groundwater level relative to the reservoir water causes the roof of the cavities within the reservoir to be unable to withstand the increased water pressure, resulting in gravitational collapse and leakage. This situation is observed in reservoirs such as the Houcun Reservoir in Renhuai County, Guizhou, and the Yuxiba Reservoir in Xishui County. 3. When the reservoir water level rises, the delayed response of the reservoir water level relative to the groundwater level causes the groundwater to support the cavity roof and leads to gas compression and expansion. This results in the subsidence of the cavity roof, establishing a connection between the reservoir water and the caverns, which leads to leakage.

After the karst reservoir is filled, the longitudinal profile along the river channel of the reservoir typically exhibits the following characteristics: there are no fracture points or steep banks in the downstream river channel of the dam; there are no longitudinal leakage low troughs on both sides of the river valley; the rock strata on both sides of the dam, both upstream and downstream, remain intact, and the karst is not developed. Such reservoirs will not experience longitudinal leakage or leakage around the dam. The longitudinal river channel may present an anomalous profile or a stepped river valley. There may be stepped fracture points, steep banks, and a significant amount of accumulation. There can be double-layered or multi-layered river channels as well as suspended river channels. After filling, such reservoirs are prone to leakage. There are longitudinal groundwater low troughs present on the bank slopes, which are exposed downstream, and karst development is observed on both sides of the dam site. These reservoirs are susceptible to leakage. In China's southwestern region, which has the largest distribution area of karst, the karst plateau is formed due to recent gaps and tectonic uplifting movements. Due to the uneven lithologic distribution and irregular karst development, the deep-cut gorges generally do not extend the karst conduits to far-distance neighboring valleys or lowlands, so there is typically no leakage towards neighboring valleys or lowlands. However, under certain special circumstances, leakage may occur, such as in some reservoirs in the Yunnan-Guizhou region that experience long-distance leakage problems through neighboring valley conduits. After the reservoir is filled, the leakage issues towards transverse neighboring valleys can be classified into five types: (1) non-groundwater divide recharge type leaking reservoirs; (2) non-groundwater divide discharge type leaking reservoirs; (3) groundwater divide with raised water level and no leakage type reservoirs; (4) groundwater divide with local raised water level and no leakage type reservoirs; (5) groundwater divide with local raised water level and leakage type reservoirs.

Reservoir leakage is one of the most complex issues in karst regions and remains a focal point of interest, directly affecting whether a reservoir can form and its economic benefits. The geomorphological conditions of the Eastern Yunnan Plateau are unique, characterized by an interspersed distribution of mountains and basins, an overall flat and open terrain with shallow cuts, and deeply incised river valleys surrounding it, with elevations dropping hundreds of meters. The structural complexity features gentle folding, steeply dipping faulting interwoven in development, with carbonate rocks widely distributed and karstification well-developed. The geological environmental conditions of the Eastern Yunnan Plateau are complex, and the hydrodynamic conditions of karst leakage in reservoirs vary significantly due to the presence of deeply incised river valleys. Moreover, most reservoirs built in the plateau area during the 1950s and 1960s exhibit issues related to karst leakage. Clearly, conducting systematic research on the karst leakage issues of hydraulic projects within such a complex context is highly meaningful. This paper, based on perspectives from systems engineering geology, deepens the understanding of the uniqueness and controlling factors of karst development in the plateau watershed and river valley slope areas, classifies the main karst hydrogeological structure types and aquifer system types in the Eastern Yunnan Plateau, and summarizes the types and characteristics of the karst groundwater flow systems within the study area. Based on hydrological and hydrogeological engineering analyses of over 20 existing problematic reservoirs in the area, it proposes a geological model for reservoir leakage in the plateau region and, in conjunction with the engineering characteristics of the proposed Heitan River Reservoir, establishes an evaluation method and process for reservoir leakage in the plateau area and conducts seepage prevention verification, thereby providing scientific support for the validation of the Heitan River hydraulic project.

The reservoir leakage in the mountainous region involves both soluble rock areas and non-soluble rock areas, with more significant leakage hazards occurring in soluble rock areas. Based on variations in leakage location, leakage media, and leakage distance, the karst leakage of reservoirs in the mountainous region can be categorized into two main types: conventional leakage type and distant-deep cutting erosion base level leakage type. The distant-deep cutting erosion base level leakage type is characterized by long leakage distances and greater relative drop, representing a unique leakage pattern within the region. For the assessment of the distant-deep cutting erosion base level leakage type in the mountainous area, the spatial distribution of the karst aquifer system and the changes in the boundaries of the karst water flow system before and after water storage are crucial evaluation factors. Notably, the connectivity between the karst aquifer system and the distant-deep regional drainage base level is a key factor in assessing the distant-deep cutting erosion base level leakage type. After normal water storage in the Heitan River reservoir, the watershed boundary between the original shallow karst water flow system and the deeper karst water flow system disappeared, resulting in a unified groundwater flow system. Leakage from the reservoir to the Lingtang underground river occurred, with a significant volume and severity. The seepage control measures for karst reservoirs should be based on actual geological conditions, selecting technically feasible and economically reasonable seepage prevention solutions. The seepage curtain scheme implemented in the Heitan River reservoir area focuses on areas of critical leakage. Numerical simulations were conducted to calculate leakage amounts and seepage control investments at different depths, along with an economic comparison, leading to a recommended curtain depth (60% of the thickness of the leaking rock mass). This provides a scientific basis for the validation of the Heitan River water conservancy project.

The construction of hydraulic engineering has rapidly developed alongside the advancement of the economy and technology in our country, pushing hydraulic projects into increasingly complex geological conditions (such as tectonic zones and karst development areas). The western mountainous regions of our country possess abundant water resources and are also areas of tectonic and karst development. Construction of hydraulic projects in the western region frequently encounters issues related to karst development. To harness the rich hydropower resources in karst areas, the first step is to address the myriad complex engineering and hydrogeological problems arising from the karst development, such as leakage problems at reservoir dam foundations, adjacent valleys, and reservoir areas due to karst features. Adverse geological phenomena, such as karst processes, significantly impact both the construction and operational phases of hydraulic projects and are key factors in the success or failure of entire projects. Therefore, investigating and determining the degree of karst development in the construction area plays a crucial role in the feasibility study period of hydraulic project construction. 

The proposed Changlang Dam Reservoir is located in the upstream region of the Liweidi River in the western mountainous area of Shidian County, belonging to a tributary of the Shidian River. The main geological formation on the right bank of the dam site consists of the Upper Renzhenhe Bridge Member (S2r) carbonate rock interbedded with clastic rocks (sandy mudstone, shale) of the Middle Silurian, which is characterized by the development of small dissolution pores, solution troughs, fissures, and minor caves; the main formation on the left bank of the dam site consists of the Upper Woniusi Member (C3w) volcanic rock of the Carboniferous, with both formations contacting each other in the valley area in a fault manner, and the fault strike is nearly vertical to the proposed dam. Once the reservoir is completed and begins to store water, karst development areas and fault fracture zones may form favorable leakage channels, which will greatly affect the economic benefits and utilization rate of the project, and may even lead to project failure. Based on field geological surveys and preliminary exploration data, a systematic analysis of the hydrogeological conditions related to karst at the Changlang Dam Reservoir site on the Liweidi River has been conducted, starting from the geological environmental conditions of the dam site. Coupled with aquifer pump tests from boreholes, the infiltration characteristics of the rock mass in the study area were briefly analyzed, and the permeability of the rock mass was calculated. The leakage conditions at the dam site, possible leakage channels, and leakage mechanisms were then discussed. Using hydrogeological analytical methods, an estimation of possible leakage volumes through potential leakage channels was conducted, followed by three-dimensional numerical simulation analysis of the groundwater flow field at the dam site using Visual MODFLOW software. The simulation results provided insights on potential leakage points and quantified leakage volumes, alongside proposed leakage prevention measures.

1) The hydrogeological conditions of the dam site area were analyzed: the groundwater types in the dam site area are divided into unconfined and confined aquifers. The confined aquifer in the study area exists in the carbonate formations of the right bank ridge, whereas unconfined groundwater is present throughout the study area. The interaction time between groundwater and rocks varies, with the carbonate rocks primarily exhibiting a calcite saturation index less than zero, indicating dominant dissolution processes; in contrast, the volcanic rocks display a calcite saturation index greater than zero, suggesting a tendency for precipitation. Preliminary hydrological chemistry analysis indicates that the main water chemistry type in the dam site area is HCO3-Ca water type.

2) Results from geophysical surveys, borehole data, and surface investigations show that the majority of the carbonate rock surface in the dam site area is covered by red clay. Exposures are mainly concentrated at the left bank ridge, where surface dissolution grooves, solution cavities, and fractures are well developed, forming a strong dissolution zone that serves as the primary pathway for groundwater flow. The area near the dam site is primarily affected by the Shunhe Fault, with well-developed fissures and small-scale faults in the strata, including solution cavities, fissures, small caves, and fractured dissolution zones, which facilitate the formation of conduits for groundwater movement.

3) Analysis of borehole pressurization experiments indicates that the permeability values of the rock mass in the right bank are generally less than 3 Lu, with local sections showing confined characteristics during drilling, suggesting relatively good rock integrity and weaker permeability; however, vertical permeability zoning is not pronounced. The permeability of limestone in the dam foundation and the left bank rock mass is relatively good, with values ranging from 4 to 5 Lu, while the permeability of basalt is around 2 Lu.

4) Based on the analysis of karst development, combined with the conditions of possible leakage pathways and the hydrodynamic conditions after reservoir impoundment, it is considered that the main leakage mode in the dam site area is fissure leakage, primarily occurring at the dam foundation and the thin ridge at the left bank of the reservoir head. The right bank ridge of the dam site features a watershed, precluding leakage into adjacent low-lying valleys. Initial calculations of leakage volumes for each pathway using hydrogeological theoretical formulas indicate that when the reservoir's water level is 1845 m, the total leakage from the dam foundation and the left bank ridge is 1328.52 m3/d; at 1840 m, the total leakage is 1037.24 m3/d; at 1835 m, the total leakage is 764.87 m3/d; and at 1830 m, the total leakage is 549.23 m3/d.

5) Numerical simulation analysis of the groundwater seepage field conducted for natural storage conditions and critical anti-seepage sections under the maximum risk water level (1845 m) shows that the total seepage volumes for the reservoir are 1406.71 m3/d and 260.45 m3/d, indicating the necessity of implementing essential anti-seepage measures to effectively reduce leakage volumes. When full-section anti-seepage measures are implemented, the lateral movement of reservoir water circumventing the anti-seepage curtain is interrupted, and vertical permeability is reduced, further decreasing leakage volumes. Simulations of leakage volumes were conducted at different water levels: at 1845 m, leakage is 138.29 m3/d; at 1840 m, leakage is 99.83 m3/d; at 1835 m, leakage is 36.04 m3/d; and at 1830 m, leakage is 25.34 m3/d.

6) The findings of the study indicate that the groundwater level in the dam site area is relatively high, with well-developed karst features in soluble rocks. Permeability is significantly greater near structural belts, combined with a relatively small catchment area and inflow, suggesting that the Longlang Dam reservoir has poor conditions for formation under natural circumstances and a low likelihood of successful reservoir formation. However, after implementing engineering anti-seepage measures, calculations indicate that a water level of 1835 m offers favorable conditions for reservoir formation and good economic benefits.

Under specific geological conditions, the elevation of the water head in a reservoir can potentially drive the stored water to seep through certain underground channels towards neighboring areas or downstream, thus reducing the reservoir's water storage capacity and impacting its operational efficiency. The development of karst features, especially the occurrence of karst conduits, significantly increases the likelihood of severe, persistent leakage from the reservoir, with the leakage volume being of considerable concern. The design normal water level for the proposed Yudonghe Reservoir is set at 599m, with a reservoir return water length of approximately 6km. The dam site and the reservoir basin are composed of soluble rocks from the Triassic system, exhibiting a strip-like NW orientation. Within this area, the karst features primarily develop in a bedded manner, characterized by dissolution fractures, voids, and caves, indicating the potential presence of pathways that could lead to reservoir leakage. Topographically, the northwestern edge of the reservoir is at a higher elevation, with the valley incision heights significantly exceeding the normal water level. At the stratigraphic boundary between the T3x and T2l formations in the southeast near Lijiahe, the valley incision elevation is 590m, and at an elevation of 598m, a large karst cave develops, which is below the reservoir’s design water level, suggesting hydrodynamic conditions and feasible pathways for leakage towards the adjacent valley. Therefore, the karst leakage issue in the reservoir and dam area is prominent. This study examines the geological environmental conditions of the research area, investigates the hydrogeological conditions and hydrodynamic characteristics of the groundwater system in the karst region, clarifies the degree and scale of surface and subsurface karst development, summarizes the primary distribution patterns of soluble rock dissolution, and analyzes the results of hydraulic tests on the rock masses in the study area. Based on this, the potential subsurface karst pathways and leakage patterns are analyzed. A hydrodynamic analysis method is employed to discuss the existence of the watershed, and the MODFLOW software is utilized to establish a groundwater seepage model for the reservoir area, simulating and predicting the existence of the watershed between the Yudonghe Reservoir and the surrounding valleys, the degree of watershed displacement post-storage, and the leakage volume under various operational conditions, ultimately proposing seepage control measures and recommendations.

The main research findings of this paper are as follows: (1) An investigation and analysis of the topography, geomorphology, stratigraphy, rock properties, geological structures, regional structural stability, and karst hydrogeological conditions of the study area were conducted, along with a study of the engineering hydrogeological conditions of the reservoir area. (2) Based on the analysis of the scale, degree of development, and distribution characteristics of karst in the study area, the karst formation mechanism and the distribution patterns of karst dissolution were studied, with a focus on the dissolution characteristics of the underground karst medium and its hydrogeological properties, clarifying the potential for the karst medium to form leakage pathways in the dam area. (3) Through the analysis of data from pressurization tests on rock mass drill holes in the study area, the permeability of the karst medium was assessed, summarizing the vertical spatial distribution characteristics of soluble rock mass infiltration in the reservoir area, which serves as a basis for model generalization and hydrological geotechnical parameter estimation. (4) An analysis of the hydrodynamic conditions for leakage from the engineering area into adjacent valleys was conducted, discussing the strength of the leakage hydrodynamic conditions, using hydrodynamic methods applicable to inter-river areas to calculate the elevation and location of the watershed, thereby clarifying the hydraulic connection between Yidong River and Lijia River. (5) This study employed a three-dimensional numerical analysis method to simulate the groundwater flow field of the study area under three different conditions: pre-reservoir filling, post-reservoir filling, and with the added impermeable curtain. The results confirmed the existence of the watershed and calculated potential leak points in the dam area, with leakage amounts determined to be: 1512.6 m³/d at the dam foundation, 761 m³/d at the left dam shoulder, and 492 m³/d at the right dam shoulder, identifying these as primary leakage points, while the total leakage for the reservoir was 2765.2 m³/d. With the installation of an impermeable curtain at a depth of 94 m below the riverbed, the total leakage reduced to 1234.5 m³/d, accounting for 2.26% of the total designed capacity of the reservoir. (6) Based on the above findings regarding the karst development characteristics in the reservoir area, the permeability characteristics of the rock mass, and the simulation analysis results of the flow field under different conditions, it is concluded that the degree of karst development in the Yidong River reservoir dam area is relatively low, which has a minor impact on the project. However, the proposed impermeable measures and recommendations for potential leakage points can ensure its normal and safe operation.

The significance of plain reservoirs in addressing local water resource shortages and uneven distribution of water resources is considerable. Research indicates that leakage from plain reservoirs is one of the main issues arising after water storage, significantly affecting the safety and stability of the reservoir, agricultural and industrial production in the reservoir area, ecological environment, and infrastructure. The geological conditions of plain reservoir areas are complex, and the causes of leakage and research methods are unique. Systematic studies on these aspects can provide theoretical foundations and technical support for the analysis of leakage causes and prevention. This paper focuses on the leakage of plain reservoirs, employing a combination of theoretical analysis and numerical simulation to reveal the causes and evolution of leakage, and proposes leakage prevention strategies, delivering theoretical and technical support for solving practical engineering problems. The research analysis addresses the following aspects: (1) Describing the characteristics of plain reservoirs, analyzing the classification of leakage, generating conditions, and influencing factors of leakage. Using Shandong Donghu Reservoir as an example, empirical data is compiled and analyzed to identify the main influencing factors of leakage in such reservoirs, discussing the leakage mechanisms of embankment foundations and dams, and studying leakage prevention strategies for plain reservoirs. (2) Taking Donghu Reservoir as an example, a two-dimensional mathematical model of the seepage field is constructed for four dam segments under five different scenarios. (3) Using the seepage calculation software Unsst2, numerical analyses of the seepage field are performed. By integrating on-site measured data from Donghu Reservoir, the changes and patterns of leakage in plain reservoirs are deduced. The leakage volume is a critical parameter for measuring the degree of leakage in plain reservoirs. Within the same mathematical model, a greater difference in upstream and downstream water levels results in an increased leakage volume. (4) Using inversion and sensitivity analysis methods, local parameters within the variable seepage field are altered based on simulations of the hydrogeological characteristics in the reservoir area to analyze the sensitivity of various parameters in the seepage field and investigate the leakage mechanisms of plain reservoirs. The analysis reveals that the seepage field is governed by the water level differences between upstream and downstream and the permeability of each region. Key influencing factors on leakage volume and seepage gradient include the permeability coefficient of the impermeable wall, the permeability coefficient and thickness of soil layers, the head difference, and the interconnection effect of groundwater wells in the reservoir area.

The seepage of plain reservoirs results from a combination of multiple factors, including engineering geomorphological conditions, hydrometeorological conditions, design and construction practices, reservoir operation management, as well as human and animal activities. These factors are closely interconnected, mutually influencing one another, and collectively affecting the plain reservoir. Therefore, a detailed analysis of the causes of seepage in plain reservoirs is necessary to provide guidance for formulating seepage control measures. Once the plain reservoir is in operation, seepage will inevitably occur; controllable seepage is considered normal. However, if the seepage pressure, hydraulic gradient, or seepage flow rate exceeds allowable limits, it is classified as abnormal seepage, or leakage. The causes of leakage can vary by location, primarily falling into categories related to dam body seepage, dam foundation seepage, and other factors. The main causes of dam body seepage include an excessively high saturation line or cracking in the dam body, allowing seepage flow to pass through the voids or fractures in the dam and exit from the downstream slope face. The reasons for an excessively high saturation line are: (1) the use of different soil materials during earthen dam construction, leading to disparities that can result in improper distribution of varying soil properties within the dam body; (2) inadequate drainage facilities behind the dam or unreasonable drainage design; (3) insufficient compaction during filling of the dam body; (4) non-compliance with gradation requirements in the downstream filter layer, or the filter layer not being constructed according to gradation specifications; (5) excessively thin dam dimensions, especially where the thickness of the inclined face or core wall is too small, causing excessive hydraulic gradients that can be breached by seepage, resulting in dam body leakage.

The leakage characteristics of reservoirs vary due to differences in topography, geological structure, and lithology. Leakage can occur in both wide and gentle terrain reservoirs as well as those located in high mountain or canyon areas. The conditions for reservoir leakage include: (1) Topographical conditions, such as the presence of a topographical saddle or an unconfined basin shape; (2) Structural conditions, where the development of faults and other geological structures damages originally low-permeability intact rock, creating leakage pathways; (3) Geological conditions, where loose sediments around the reservoir and at the reservoir bottom have high permeability, potentially resulting in extensive or band-like leakage, and where soluble rock is present, karst leakage may occur; (4) Hydrogeological conditions, where the reservoir basin lacks an effective aquiclude or a relatively confined aquiclude, and the groundwater level is lower than the normal water storage level, could indicate the presence of reservoir leakage. Based on the location of leakage, it can be classified into two categories: leakage in the reservoir area and leakage in the dam area.

(1) Reservoir area leakage. The conditions for reservoir area leakage include: (1) In terms of landform, the presence of low adjacent valleys or depressions around the reservoir whose bottom elevation is lower than the normal water storage level; (2) The existence of permeable geological materials that connect the inside and outside of the reservoir, distributed at elevations lower than the normal water storage level, such as ancient river channels or glacial deposits of gravel and sand, thin watersheds, thick weathered bedrock layers, zones of intense fracturing, fault zones, and karst voids or caves. There are primarily two scenarios for reservoir area leakage: one involves leakage from the shoreline or reservoir bottom across the landform watershed into adjacent low valleys or distant depressions, which is considered inter-basin leakage, while the other occurs through meandering river sections (the landform watershed) leaking downstream of the dam, termed meander segment leakage.

(2) Dam area leakage. Dam area leakage can be further divided based on specific leakage locations into leakage at the dam foundation and leakage around the dam. Dam foundation leakage refers to leakage that occurs after the reservoir is filled, due to the hydraulic head difference between the upstream and downstream, causing water within the reservoir to seep downstream through the pores, cracks, faults, caverns, and other pathways within the dam foundation geological materials. This leakage often occurs where seepage control measures have not been implemented or are inadequate, reducing the effectiveness of the reservoir, increasing the uplift pressure on the dam base, and potentially leading to internal erosion and instability of the dam foundation. Leakage around the dam, also known as shoulder leakage, refers to the seepage of reservoir water downstream along the geological materials on both sides of the dam due to the head difference between the upstream and downstream.

In recent years, with the rapid development of China's national economy, the construction and development of pump storage hydropower stations in China have also accelerated significantly, achieving substantial progress during this period. Pump storage hydropower stations are facilities that can both pump water and generate electricity. They consist of upper and lower reservoirs, utilizing surplus electrical energy from the power system during specific periods to pump water from the lower reservoir to the upper reservoir, thereby storing energy in the form of potential energy. When needed, water is released from the upper reservoir back to the lower reservoir for power generation. The pump storage hydropower stations have made a tremendous contribution to the development of hydropower energy in China, and the feasibility and safety evaluation of constructing these stations in karst regions under complex geological conditions is increasingly being emphasized. 

The proposed site for the Zhen'an Pump Storage Hydropower Station is located about 47 km northwest of Zhen'an County, near the border with NingShan County, approximately 40 km north of NingShan County. The station consists of upper and lower reservoirs. The upper reservoir's first phase is situated in the right bank tributary of the Yuehe River—Jin Pengou, with a storage capacity of 1,000 × 10^4 m³ and a normal water level of 1390 m. The total installed capacity of the station is 2400 MW. Due to the complex geological conditions in the karst region, classified as unfavorable geological formations, it is necessary to study the safety and stability conditions of the karst reservoir area of the proposed Zhen'an Pump Storage Hydropower Station project. The second phase of the upper reservoir and the lower reservoir are located in an intrusive rock zone with relatively favorable geological conditions; hence this article focuses on a detailed analysis of karst leakage for the upper reservoir's first phase. 

Starting from the geological environmental conditions of the study area, this article investigates the hydrogeological conditions and hydrodynamic characteristics of the groundwater system in the karst area, clarifies the development degree and scale of surface and underground karst, summarizes the main distribution patterns of soluble rock dissolution, and evaluates the spatial permeability distribution characteristics of the karst medium. Based on this foundation, an analysis of potential underground karst pathways and various leakage patterns is conducted, studying possible leakage locations in the dam area. Subsequently, numerical simulations of the reservoir area are carried out using MODFLOW software to calculate the potential leakage locations' risk and the volume of water leakage from the reservoir, thereby further assessing the impact of karst reservoir leakage.

The main research achievements of this paper are as follows: (1) An investigation and analysis of the topography, geomorphology, stratigraphy, lithology, geological structure, regional tectonic stability, and karst hydrogeological conditions in the study area were conducted, along with a study of the engineering hydrogeological conditions of the reservoir area. (2) Based on the analysis of the scale, development degree, and distribution characteristics of karst in the study area, combined with laboratory experimental results, research on the formation mechanism of karst and the distribution patterns of dissolution was conducted, focusing on the dissolution characteristics of the underground karst medium and its hydrogeological properties, elucidating the potential for the formation of karst leakage pathways in the reservoir dam area. (3) Through the analysis of pump test data from boreholes in the study area and the calculation of permeability tensors, the permeability of the karst medium was analyzed, summarizing the spatial distribution characteristics of soluble rock bodies in horizontal and vertical directions, providing a basis for model generalization. (4) Based on an analysis of the existing leakage pathways and hydrodynamic conditions in the reservoir dam area, a focused study was conducted on potential leakage sites, particularly at thin ridges that submerge underground watersheds and at locations of severe dissolution in the dam foundation and abutments. (5) This paper utilized three-dimensional numerical analysis methods to simulate the groundwater seepage field in the study area under three different conditions: pre-reservoir impoundment, post-impoundment, and with the implementation of seepage control curtains. The calculated leakage rates for several potential key leakage points after reservoir impoundment are as follows: dam foundation leakage 713.63 m³/d, left abutment leakage 115.79 m³/d, thin ridge A leakage 131.46 m³/d, with these being the main leakage points, and the total leakage from the reservoir being 1140.37 m³/d. (6) Based on the aforementioned characteristics of karst development in the reservoir area, the permeability characteristics of the rock mass, and the simulation analysis results of the seepage field under different conditions, it is concluded that the degree of karst development in the Zhen'an pumped storage power station reservoir dam area is relatively low, with minimal impact on the project; the proposed seepage control measures and recommendations for potential leakage sites can ensure its normal and safe operation.
